title: International migration: December 2018 | Stats NZ
content:
## International migration: December 2018
15 February 2019, 10:45am
International migration statistics give the latest outcomes-based measure of migration, which includes estimates of migrants entering or leaving New Zealand.
- International migration: December 2018 Excel spreadsheet, 88 KB

### International migration: December 2018
Excel spreadsheet, 88 KB
- International migration: December 2018 – citizenship by visa, country last permanent residence – CSV Comma Separated Values, 6 MB

### International migration: December 2018 – citizenship by visa, country last permanent residence – CSV
Comma Separated Values, 6 MB
- International migration: December 2018 – estimated migration by age sex – CSV Comma Separated Values, 681 KB

### International migration: December 2018 – estimated migration by age sex – CSV
Comma Separated Values, 681 KB
The comparisons between the December 2017 and 2018 month and year use the latest provisional estimates for each period.
Note: Information on arrivals intentions is still collected from the passenger on the arrival card. This is used for international travel information (see International travel: December 2018 ), but it is not used in the new official outcomes measure of migration.
Subnational information is not available for this December 2018 release. It will be included in later international migration releases.
The provisional estimates have 95 percent confidence intervals (±) alongside them – the wider the interval, the greater the uncertainty about the estimate. However, these intervals reflect the model uncertainty, not the extent of future revisions to provisional data.
Provisional migration estimates are revised each month until they are finalised after 16 months.
Year ended December 2018 (compared with December 2017 year) provisional estimates:
- migrant arrivals – 145,800 (± 1,700), up 2 percent
- migrant departures – 97,500 (± 1,400), up 9 percent
- annual net migration – 48,300 (± 1,800), down from 52,700 (± 200).
For migrant arrivals, in the December 2018 year New Zealand citizens were the largest group with 36,500 (± 600) arrivals. The next largest groups were:
- China – 15,700 (± 400)
- India – 14,400 (± 200)
- United Kingdom – 7,800 (± 200)
- Philippines – 7,600 (± 100)
- Australia – 7,400 (± 500).
For migrant departures, in the December 2018 year New Zealand citizens were the largest group with 44,700 (± 900) departures. The next largest groups were:
- China – 10,500 (± 400)
- United Kingdom – 5,900 (± 200)
- India – 5,400 (± 200)
- Australia – 4,000 (± 300).
December 2018 quarter (compared with December 2017 quarter) provisional estimates:
- migrant arrivals – 40,300 (± 1500), up 8 percent
- migrant departures – 28,100 (± 1200), up 15 percent
- net gain – 12,200 (± 1,600) migrants.
December 2018 month (compared with December 2017 month) provisional estimates:
- migrant arrivals – 14,900 (± 1200), up 12 percent
- migrant departures – 11,900 (± 1,000), up 13 percent
- net gain – 3,100 (± 1,300) migrants.
December 2018 month seasonally adjusted estimates:
- migrant arrivals – 13,700
- migrant departures – 8,600
- net gain – 5,100 migrants.
Seasonally adjusted monthly migration arrivals and departures, June 2014 to December 2018
Monthly net migration (mean estimate), June 2014 to December 2018

### Outcomes-based measure of migration
The outcomes-based measure of migration with final and provisional estimates is now the official way we measure migration in New Zealand. The results build on the outcomes-based measure of migration released from May 2017 and enabled the removal of the departure card.
The new approach uses passport data to link arrivals and departures and accurately measure how long people spend in, or out of, New Zealand after their initial border crossing.
To classify a border crossing as a migrant movement, we need to observe up to 16 months of travel history.
A traveller who did not live here previously is considered a long-term migrant arrival if they have spent 12 of the following 16 months in New Zealand. A traveller who previously lived in New Zealand, but who spent 12 of the next 16 months out of New Zealand is classified as a long-term migrant departure.
With this new approach it takes 17 months before final migration estimates are available. To produce timely results, we use a statistical model to produce provisional migration estimates. Statistics produced using these provisional estimates have uncertainty for 16 months; after this time we can finalise the classification of all border crossings.
Data in more recent months has more uncertainty and while older months are more certain. We will revise provisional estimates regularly until they are finalised 17 months after the reference period. At this point the estimates change from being provisional to being final estimates.
Of the 14 million border crossings in the December 2018 year, 81 percent are currently classified with certainty. The remaining 19 percent represent 2.6 million border crossings, so a small change can affect the migration estimates.
The migration estimates become more certain after each subsequent month. In the December month, 1 in 4 arrivals are classified with certainty. This increases to 9 in 10 after four months. Therefore we expect the monthly revisions to become relatively small after about five months, as we can calculate the duration of stay/absence more definitively (according to the 12/16-month rule ).
Our model will evolve as we get more data.  A key next step is to take the new series further back in time to quantify the variability in the provisional estimates.
A table that summarises the differences in percent modelled between November results from the release last month and this month is available on request (email demography@stats.govt.nz ).

### Outcomes-based and intentions-based measures compared
The previous migration measure (permanent and long-term (PLT) migration) was estimated from travellers’ statements on arrival or departure cards – based on how long they intended to stay in New Zealand (or be away). This intentions-based measure was timely; however, traveller behaviour was not always consistent with the stated intentions at their border crossing. This may be due to: circumstances changing, misunderstanding the questions on the traveller cards and incorrectly reporting their intentions, or deciding to extend their visa or stay/absence.
The outcomes-based measure is estimated from the actual travel histories of people travelling in and out of New Zealand. This provides a more accurate measure of migration – whether they are a migrant is based on their actual movements.
Provisional outcomes arrival and departure estimates are consistently higher than intentions PLT estimates.
Provisional outcomes net migration estimates are sometimes higher, and sometimes lower, than intentions PLT estimates. In recent years, net migration using outcomes has been lower than intentions.
Text alternative for figure 3, Migration estimates (thousands) by direction, rolling annual, year ended December 2001 to October 2018
The overall trend of migrant arrivals and departures is similar for both measures. Although there are differences between migrant estimates when comparing outcomes with intentions, both show significant rises and falls in the number of migrants for similar periods.
Outcomes versus intentions: Measuring migration based on travel histories has more information that compares the two measures.
See Migration Data Transformation or email info@stats.govt.nz for more information.

Subject category: Tourism Group: International travel and migration – ITM

## Definitions and metadata
International migration – DataInfo+ gives general methodology used to produce international travel and migration statistics.
International migration concepts – DataInfo+ gives definitions of terms used in this release.
Subnational short-term NZ-resident arrivals – DataInfo+ gives methodology for determining the New Zealand-location information in short-term New Zealand-resident arrival records.

### Text alternative for figure 3
Three time-series line graphs showing migration estimates for migrant arrivals, migrant departures, and net migration, from rolling annual years ended December 2001 to October 2018 – for experimental series, new series, and intentions-based series.
The overall trend of migrant arrivals and departures is similar for the outcomes and intentions measures.
